title: Introduction to the Tidyverse
description: Learn dplyr and ggplot2 fundamentals through hands-on practice with a Socratic AI pair partner.

tasks.title: Your first tibble
tasks.instructions:
A **tibble** is the tidyverse's modern take on a data frame. It prints cleanly and behaves predictably.

Create a tibble called `students` with three columns:
- `name`: character ŌĆö `"Alice"`, `"Bob"`, `"Carol"`, `"David"`
- `age`: numeric ŌĆö `22`, `19`, `24`, `21`
- `score`: numeric ŌĆö `88`, `74`, `95`, `61`

Hint: use `tibble()` from the `tibble` package (already loaded).
tasks.starter_code:
# Create your tibble here
students <- tibble(
  
)

# Print it
students
tasks.solution_code:
students <- tibble(
  name  = c("Alice", "Bob", "Carol", "David"),
  age   = c(22, 19, 24, 21),
  score = c(88, 74, 95, 61)
)
students
tasks.test_expression: is.data.frame(students) && ncol(students) == 3 && nrow(students) == 4 && all(c('name','age','score') %in% names(students)) && isTRUE(students$score[students$name == 'Alice'] == 88) && isTRUE(students$score[students$name == 'Carol'] == 95) && isTRUE(students$age[students$name == 'Bob'] == 19) && isTRUE(students$age[students$name == 'David'] == 21)
hints: Inside tibble(), each column is name = vector.
Use c() to create a vector of values: c("Alice", "Bob", ...).
The column names should be exactly: name, age, score.
tasks.ai_context: Task is about creating a tibble with tibble(). Student should use name=, age=, score= columns. Encourage them to notice how tibble printing differs from base data.frame.

tasks.title: Filtering rows with filter()
tasks.instructions:
`filter()` keeps only rows that match a condition.

Using the `students` tibble from the previous task, use `filter()` to create a new tibble called `high_scorers` containing only students with a `score` above 80.

Then print `high_scorers`.
tasks.starter_code:
# You should have students from the previous task.
# If not, re-run task 1 first.

high_scorers <- filter(students, )

high_scorers
hints: filter(data, condition) ŌĆö the condition uses column names directly, no $.
The comparison operator for 'greater than' is >.
You want rows where score > 80, which should give you 2 students.
tasks.ai_context: Task is about filter(). Student should write filter(students, score > 80). Common mistake: using students$score inside filter ŌĆö not needed with dplyr. Encourage them to try other conditions too.

tasks.title: Selecting columns with select()
tasks.instructions:
`select()` picks (or drops) columns by name.

From the `students` tibble, create a new tibble called `name_score` that contains **only** the `name` and `score` columns.

Print the result.
hints: select(data, col1, col2, ...) ŌĆö just list the column names you want.
You want two columns: name and score. That's all.
You can also drop columns with a minus sign: select(data, -age).
tasks.ai_context: Task is about select(). Expected answer: select(students, name, score). Encourage exploration of the - (drop) syntax as a bonus.

tasks.title: Adding columns with mutate()
tasks.instructions:
`mutate()` adds new columns (or transforms existing ones), keeping all existing columns too.

Add a new column called `grade` to the `students` tibble. The grade rules are:
- score Ōēź 90 ŌåÆ `"A"`
- score Ōēź 80 ŌåÆ `"B"`
- score Ōēź 70 ŌåÆ `"C"`
- otherwise ŌåÆ `"D"`

Save the result as `students_graded` and print it.

*Tip: `case_when()` pairs beautifully with `mutate()` for multi-condition logic.*
tasks.solution_code:
students_graded <- mutate(
  students,
  grade = case_when(
    score >= 90 ~ "A",
    score >= 80 ~ "B",
    score >= 70 ~ "C",
    TRUE        ~ "D"
  )
)
students_graded
hints: case_when() syntax: condition ~ value, next_condition ~ next_value, ...
Conditions are checked in order, top to bottom ŌĆö put the strictest first.
Use TRUE ~ "D" as the final 'catch-all' case (like an else).
score >= 90 ~ "A", score >= 80 ~ "B", score >= 70 ~ "C", TRUE ~ "D"
tasks.ai_context: Task is about mutate() and case_when(). Key insight: case_when conditions are evaluated in order, so >= 90 before >= 80 works correctly. TRUE ~ 'D' is the catch-all. Alice has 88 so should get B. Carol has 95 so should get A.

tasks.title: Chaining steps with the pipe |>
tasks.instructions:
The native pipe `|>` (R 4.1+) lets you chain operations left-to-right, so you can read transformations in the order they happen.

Rewrite the following two-step operation **as a single pipe chain** ŌĆö no intermediate variables:

```r
step1 <- filter(students, age >= 21)
step2 <- select(step1, name, score)
```

Save the result as `result` and print it.
tasks.solution_code:
result <- students |>
  filter(age >= 21) |>
  select(name, score)
result
tasks.test_expression: exists('result') && nrow(result) == 3 && ncol(result) == 2 && all(c('name','score') %in% names(result)) && all(c('Alice','Carol','David') %in% result$name) && !('Bob' %in% result$name)
hints: The pipe passes the left-hand side as the first argument to the right-hand function.
students |> filter(...) is the same as filter(students, ...).
Chain two steps: filter() then select().
result <- students |> filter(age >= 21) |> select(name, score)
tasks.ai_context: Task introduces the native pipe |>. The equivalent of: filter(students, age >= 21) then select(name, score). There are 3 students aged 21+: Alice(22), Carol(24), David(21). Result should have 3 rows and 2 columns.

tasks.title: Summarising with group_by() and summarise()
tasks.instructions:
`group_by()` + `summarise()` is dplyr's split-apply-combine ŌĆö it groups your data, applies a summary function to each group, and collapses the result.

Using `students_graded` (from task 4), calculate the **mean score** for each grade group.

The result should have two columns: `grade` and `mean_score`. Save it as `grade_summary` and print it.
tasks.starter_code:
# Make sure students_graded exists (re-run task 4 if needed)

grade_summary <- students_graded |>
  group_by() |>
  summarise(
    
  )

grade_summary
tasks.solution_code:
grade_summary <- students_graded |>
  group_by(grade) |>
  summarise(mean_score = mean(score))
grade_summary
hints: group_by(column_name) splits the data into groups.
summarise(new_col = aggregation_function(col)) collapses each group to one row.
mean(score) computes the average of the score column within each group.
group_by(grade) |> summarise(mean_score = mean(score))
tasks.ai_context: Task is group_by() + summarise(). Students should write group_by(grade) |> summarise(mean_score = mean(score)). students_graded has grades A(Carol=95), B(Alice=88), D(David=61, Bob=74 -> actually Bob=74 is C, David=61 is D). Let the student figure out the exact groups.

tasks.title: Your first ggplot
tasks.instructions:
`ggplot2` builds plots in layers. The recipe is:
1. `ggplot(data, aes(x = ..., y = ...))` ŌĆö set up the canvas and aesthetics
2. `+ geom_*()` ŌĆö add a geometry layer (points, bars, linesŌĆ”)

Create a **scatter plot** of the built-in `mtcars` dataset:
- x-axis: `wt` (car weight)
- y-axis: `mpg` (miles per gallon)
- Add a title: `"Weight vs Fuel Efficiency"`

Colour the points by `cyl` (number of cylinders) ŌĆö make `cyl` a factor so ggplot treats it as discrete.
tasks.starter_code:
ggplot(mtcars, aes(x = , y = , colour = factor( ))) +
  geom_point() +
  labs(title = " ")
tasks.solution_code:
ggplot(mtcars, aes(x = wt, y = mpg, colour = factor(cyl))) +
  geom_point(size = 2) +
  labs(
    title  = "Weight vs Fuel Efficiency",
    x      = "Weight (1000 lbs)",
    y      = "Miles per Gallon",
    colour = "Cylinders"
  )
hints: aes() maps data columns to visual properties: x, y, colour, size, shape, etc.
geom_point() draws points ŌĆö no extra arguments needed to start.
factor(cyl) converts the numeric cylinder count to a categorical variable, giving distinct colours.
labs(title = "...") adds a title. x = and y = add axis labels.
tasks.ai_context: Task is a first ggplot scatter plot. Minimum viable answer: ggplot(mtcars, aes(x=wt, y=mpg, colour=factor(cyl))) + geom_point() + labs(title='Weight vs Fuel Efficiency'). Encourage the learner to explore adding labs() for axis labels and experimenting with size, alpha in geom_point().
